dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for chest pain at --Address Redacted-- in [CITY].
Hello, I'm Alex and I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What brought you to call 911 today?

I've been having chest pain (CP) and shortness of breath (SOB) since last night around 9 PM. It woke me up around 4 AM, but I took some nitroglycerin to relieve the pain. It returned again around 9 AM this morning.
Are you alert and oriented to person, place, and time, ma'am?
Yes, I know where I am. I'm not entirely sure of the date though, maybe the 16th?
That's okay. What is your name, and what happened today that caused you to call 911?
My name is Linda. I started having chest pain and trouble breathing, which got worse this morning. I felt like I couldn't wait any longer.
Linda, can you speak to me clearly and take a deep breath for me? Do you feel like there's something blocking your throat or airway?
I can speak clearly, and I don't feel like anything is blocking my throat, but it's hard to breathe fully.
Can you help me take a look at your chest? We can clamp on a monitor to see how you're breathing.
Respirations are 24, regular. Chest rise and fall is equal bilaterally.
Pulse is 103, regular, with palpable and strong peripheral pulses. Blood pressure is 122 over 76. The skin is pale, warm, and diaphoretic.
Is there any pain or discomfort that's like in your arm or jaw? And do you have any shortness of breath or coughing?
The pain radiates into my left arm. I also had a non-productive cough all day, and yes, I feel like I can't take full breaths.
Linda, can you tell me if you have any history with heart-related issues or surgeries in the past?
I had a quadruple bypass a while back, but I can't remember exactly when.
Were you taking any medications before you called 911 or at the time of your symptoms?
I took x1 baby ASA (81 mg) and then took x4 baby ASA (81 mg each) at the instruction of 911 dispatch. I also took some nitroglycerin earlier this morning.
Tell us a bit more about what happened around the time you first started experiencing the chest pain last night. What were you doing?
I was resting in bed, just sleeping. I thought maybe it was just heartburn, but it kept getting worse. I got up and took the nitro, and the pain went away again before returning this morning.
When did your chest pain start again in the morning, and what were you doing at that time?
It came back this morning around 9 AM when I was still in bed. I wasn't doing anything physical. It was sudden and made it hard to sit up.
How would you describe your chest pain right now? Is it crushing or like something is pressing on your chest?
It's really crushing, and it feels like something is squeezing my heart.
Has the pain moved or spread anywhere else besides the chest and arm?
No, it just stays in my chest and arm.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how severe is your chest pain right now?
It's a 9. It's hard to even think anymore it's so bad.
Respirations remain at 24 per minute, no change noted.
Pulse is now 94 beats per minute, still regular and strong.
Blood pressure is 127 over 84. Pulse ox is at 96.
Pulse ox is now 93.
When we first arrived, your pulse ox was 97, and we have been keeping that close during our care.
I'm performing a 12-lead ECG to get more detailed information about your heart rhythm and confirm the acute cardiac issue we suspect.
CV - ECG - 12 Lead Obtained. The initial interpretation showed STEMI-like findings, which indicates a possible heart attack.
CV - ECG - 4 Lead Monitoring has been initiated to track your cardiac rhythms continuously as we prepare for transport.
You took one dose of 324 mg of baby aspirin (ASA) per dispatch instructions, prior to our arrival. We will not give you anything additional.
EMS attempted IV access with a 22-gauge catheter, but it was unsuccessful.
Your eyes and head appear fine, and there are no signs of trauma or swelling.
I was just resting in bed so there's nothing else there.
Linda, we're going to perform a more detailed assessment of your heart and lungs now. Let me listen to your lungs closely, okay?
Sure, go ahead.
Lung sounds are clear in all fields. No adventitious breath sounds like crackles or wheezes.
Your skin is pale and sweaty. Is there any other sign of sores or rashes?
No, nothing else. That's the only way I feel right now. My cough only started within the last 24 hours but it never produced anything.
We are taking you under the STEMI (ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction) protocol due to your symptoms and ECG findings.
Pulse is 115, regular. Blood pressure is 126 over 86, respiratory rate is 24 again. SpO₂ is now 93.
Glucose readings were attempted but could not be captured due to equipment limitations.
Has the severity of your pain changed at all since you first started experiencing it this morning?
It's about the same. I don't feel any better or worse. Still a 9 out of 10.
We are preparing to transport you to [COMPANY_NAME] in [CITY]. They have the appropriate teams for handling patients with chest pain and suspected heart attacks. Is that okay with you, ma'am?
I'm now securing you on the stretcher with a five-point seat harness and placing you on the stretcher for transport.
We'll make sure to keep you safe and comfortable during transport. Just relax and try to stay calm. If at any time you feel worse, let us know immediately.
Patient is being wheeled into the ED. We placed her on the ED bed via slidable transfer. We've handed the patient over to the ED nurse.
Patient has a diagnosis of chest pain (CP) related to suspected STEMI, with a shortness of breath (SOB). She has a history of CABG. Pain is 9 out of 10, localized in chest and left arm.
Aspirin (324 mg total) and nitroglycerin were administered as instructed by 911 prior to arrival. IV access was attempted but unsuccessful.
She remains alert, oriented, and able to answer questions coherently. She had tested for the flu and coronavirus; the flu was negative, and the coronavirus test results are pending.
Emergency medical services provided timely care, and she's ready for further evaluation from the emergency department staff. We'll stay with her until the handoff is complete.
ED nurse is now taking full responsibility for the patient. All information has been shared and transferred per facility protocol.
Our job here is complete. She's in good hands now at the ED. I hope she gets the help and care she needs fast and soon. This kind of chest pain definitely needs prompt attention in the hospital.
